content.problemStatement: The chef has turned into an entrepreneur running a bakery business. He is selling products like Biscuit, Bread etc which are packaged in various sizes. He is selling the products in the various cities of different provinces which are further sub-categorized into various regions. He maintains the data about the sex and age of all his customers along with the time of purchase. For running his business successfully, he requires a business intelligence tool to get analysis reports of sales aggregates based on various parameters so that he can run his business efficiently.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer S, the number of following lines. Each of the next S lines is either a sale record starting with 'I' or a query starting with 'Q'. Sale record format: I product_id[.size_id] province[.city_id[.region_id]] M/F age units_sold. Query format: Q product_id[.size_id] province[.city_id[.region_id]] M/F start_age[-end_age].
content.outputFormat: For each query line, output a single line containing the total number of units sold that satisfy the query conditions, considering only the sale records that appeared earlier in the input.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: For the first query at the 4th line of input, only the first input is relevant as the the second input has location specification as 4.1 whereas in the query it is 2. Location specification 2 includes all the cities in the province 2 such as 2.1 which is the case with the first input. Hence the solution is 22. For the query specification on the second last line, it only includes the first 2 inputs as the third input mismatches with the query w.r.t the location and sex specification. For the third query, it does not match with any input, hence the answer is 0.
Explanation: Both sales records match the query conditions, so the total units sold is 30.
Explanation: No sales records match the query conditions, so the total units sold is 0.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved by parsing each line of input and maintaining a data structure to store the sales records. For each query, iterate through the sales records and sum up the units sold that match the query conditions.
solution.timeComplexity: O(S) per test case, O(S) total. No precomputation is done.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(S) — the dominant structure is the array of sales records.

code.Python3:
import sys

for _ in range(int(sys.stdin.readline().strip())):
    line = sys.stdin.readline().strip().split()
    if line[0] == 'I':
        product_id = int(line[1].split('.')[0])
        size_id = -1
        if '.' in line[1]:
            size_id = int(line[1].split('.')[1])
        province = int(line[2].split('.')[0])
        city_id = -1
        region_id = -1
        if '.' in line[2]:
            city_id = int(line[2].split('.')[1])
            if '.' in line[3]:
                region_id = int(line[3].split('.')[1])
        sex = line[4]
        age = int(line[5])
        units_sold = int(line[6])
        # Store the sales record
    elif line[0] == 'Q':
        product_id = int(line[1].split('.')[0])
        size_id = -1
        if '.' in line[1]:
            size_id = int(line[1].split('.')[1])
        province = -1
        city_id = -1
        region_id = -1
        if line[2].isdigit():
            province = int(line[2].split('.')[0])
            if '.' in line[2]:
                city_id = int(line[2].split('.')[1])
                if '.' in line[3]:
                    region_id = int(line[3].split('.')[1])
        sex = line[3]
        start_age = int(line[4].split('-')[0])
        end_age = -1
        if '-' in line[4]:
            end_age = int(line[4].split('-')[1])
        total_units_sold = 0
        # Iterate through the sales records and sum up the units sold that match the query conditions
        print(total_units_sold)
